did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, to classify skin cancer images. The authors developed a fusion model (CNN+LSTM) to detect skin cancer, achieving an accuracy of 93.4% on a dataset of 10,000 skin images from Kaggle.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design and implementation of a fusion model (CNN and LSTM) for skin cancer detection. It includes specific sections on Methodology, Experiments and Results, and reports new empirical findings, such as achieving 90.2% accuracy with CNN and 93.4% with the fusion model on a dataset of 10,000 images from Kaggle. Although it contains a literature survey, its primary purpose is to report new technical results rather than summarize prior literature.

title: Innovative Way of Identifying Skin Cancer Model Design with FCNN and LSTM
journal: 2024 IEEE International Conference on Computing, Power and Communication Technologies (IC2PCT)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin cancer detection by developing a deep learning fusion model combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks. The authors utilized a balanced dataset of 10,000 skin images sourced from Kaggle, comprising 5,500 benign and 4,500 malignant samples. The methodology involved first applying a standard CNN architecture, which achieved an accuracy of 90.2%. Subsequently, a novel fusion model integrating CNN for spatial feature extraction and LSTM for temporal pattern recognition was implemented. The experimental results demonstrated that the proposed CNN-LSTM fusion model outperformed the standalone CNN, achieving a higher detection accuracy of 93.4%.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as benign/normal in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 5,500 benign images.

Classified as malignant in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 4,500 malignant samples. The dataset source URL in references mentions 'melanoma', but the paper itself uses the general terms 'malignant' and 'skin cancer' for the experimental classes.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin images into benign (normal) or malignant (skin cancer) classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to detect skin cancer. The dataset consists of 5,500 benign (normal) and 4,500 malignant samples. The model outputs a binary classification using a final dense layer with a sigmoid activation function. The primary metric reported is accuracy (90.2% for CNN and 93.4% for the CNN-LSTM fusion model).

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset sourced from Kaggle (specifically the dataset by hasnainjaved titled 'melanoma-skin-cancer-dataset-of-10000-images'), containing 10,000 skin images. The paper describes it as a balanced dataset comprising normal (benign) and malignant samples.

use.training: The dataset was partitioned into training and testing parts for model development.
use.testing: The dataset was partitioned into training and testing parts for model evaluation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared model performance using accuracy metrics (90.2% for CNN and 93.4% for the fusion model) without statistical significance analysis.

Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset.notes: The dataset comprised 10,000 images (5,500 normal/benign and 4,500 malignant). The CNN-LSTM fusion model outperformed the standalone CNN model. No standard deviations, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported.

architecture_used.main_model: Fusion of Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)

architecture_details.base_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for spatial feature extraction
architecture_details.fusion_component: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) for temporal pattern recognition
final_layers: Final dense layer with sigmoid activation

conclusions: The integration of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks into a fusion model improves the accuracy of skin cancer detection compared to using a standalone CNN.
The proposed CNN-LSTM fusion model achieved a detection accuracy of 93.4% on a balanced dataset of 10,000 skin images (5,500 benign and 4,500 malignant) sourced from Kaggle.
The standalone CNN model achieved an accuracy of 90.2% on the same dataset, serving as a baseline for comparison.
The fusion model leverages CNNs for extracting spatial features from skin lesion images and LSTMs for capturing temporal dependencies, allowing for a more comprehensive understanding of the data.

The study demonstrates that deep learning models can effectively assist in automating skin cancer diagnosis, addressing the subjectivity and time-consumption associated with traditional visual inspection by dermatologists.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single, publicly available Kaggle dataset (10,000 images) without external validation, limiting generalizability to diverse clinical populations.

The dataset is described as 'balanced' but contains an unequal class distribution (5,500 benign vs. 4,500 malignant), which may bias the model towards the majority class despite the authors' claim of balance.
The paper reports only accuracy metrics (90.2% for CNN, 93.4% for CNN-LSTM) and omits other critical diagnostic metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, or AUC-ROC.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the 3.2% performance improvement of the fusion model over the baseline CNN.

The application of LSTM networks, which are designed for sequential data, to static skin lesion images is methodologically questionable unless the input is explicitly framed as a sequence (e.g., time-series of images), which is not clearly justified for standard dermoscopy.

The paper does not report data leakage prevention strategies, such as ensuring patient-level separation between training and testing sets, raising concerns about overfitting to specific individuals in the dataset.
No details are provided regarding data preprocessing, augmentation techniques, or handling of class imbalance beyond the initial dataset composition.
The model architecture details are sparse, lacking information on hyperparameters, optimizer settings, loss functions, and training duration, hindering reproducibility.
